category_summary:
The AI community was consumed by an extraordinary political-corporate crisis. **Sam Altman** [announced **OpenAI's** agreement](/?date=2026-03-01&category=social#item-8b96285a729a) to deploy models on **Department of War** classified networks (31M+ views), while **Anthropic** issued an official response to **Secretary Hegseth's** threat to designate them a supply chain risk (12.5M views). **OpenAI** took the remarkable step of [publicly defending **Anthropic**](/?date=2026-03-01&category=social#item-62e3abfb4158) against the government action.

- **Gary Marcus** drove intense backlash, framing **Altman** as negotiating the DoW deal while publicly supporting **Dario Amodei**, [calling it a "scam"](/?date=2026-03-01&category=social#item-19cbaa8c1382) with the "fix in from the start"
- **Natolambert** [highlighted a stark cultural divide](/?date=2026-03-01&category=social#item-b70be4f0a840): every **Anthropic** employee amplified company messaging while none supported **Altman's** move
- **Ethan Mollick** [offered a measured warning](/?date=2026-03-01&category=social#item-938f38959d1f) that the episode revealed dangerously broken institutional processes for AI governance
- **Timnit Gebru** criticized both companies, noting **Anthropic** knowingly partnered with the Pentagon and **Palantir**

Amid the controversy, **Anthropic's** **Claude** [hit #1 in the App Store](/?date=2026-03-01&category=social#item-f701f4ea7186) (announced by CPO **Mike Krieger**), suggesting a Streisand effect. On the product side, **Boris Cherny** [announced major **Claude Code** features](/?date=2026-03-01&category=social#item-3b1a4e4fbd97) — /simplify and /batch — enabling parallel agent execution for code migrations and quality improvements, drawing massive developer engagement.

  • Gary Marcus drove intense backlash, framing Altman as negotiating the DoW deal while publicly supporting Dario Amodei, calling it a "scam" with the "fix in from the start"

  • Ethan Mollick offered a measured warning that the episode revealed dangerously broken institutional processes for AI governance
  • Timnit Gebru criticized both companies, noting Anthropic knowingly partnered with the Pentagon and Palantir

Amid the controversy, Anthropic's Claude hit #1 in the App Store (announced by CPO Mike Krieger), suggesting a Streisand effect. On the product side, Boris Cherny announced major Claude Code features — /simplify and /batch — enabling parallel agent execution for code migrations and quality improvements, drawing massive developer engagement.

themes.name: Anthropic Government Controversy

Anthropic is holding firm on principled grounds. Jeremy Howard is vocally criticizing the government action and those (including OpenAI employees like Joshua Achiam) who support it. Multiple Anthropic employees expressing solidarity and pride. The controversy involves Ukraine-related policy. Despite the crisis, Claude reached #1 in the App Store, suggesting a Streisand effect.

Tunguz adds sarcastic commentary about surveillance and military AI. Timnit Gebru provides a strong counter-narrative arguing Anthropic is not sympathetic given its existing Palantir/Pentagon partnerships.

items.title: Tonight, we reached an agreement with the Department of War to deploy our models in their classified...
items.content:
Tonight, we reached an agreement with the Department of War to deploy our models in their classified network.

In all of our interactions, the DoW displayed a deep respect for safety and a desire to partner to achieve the best possible outcome.

AI safety and wide distribution of benefits are the core of our mission. Two of our most important safety principles are prohibitions on domestic mass surveillance and human responsibility for the use of force, including for autonomous weapon systems.  The DoW agrees with these principles, reflects them in law and policy, and we put them into our agreement.

We also will build technical safeguards to ensure our models behave as they should, which the DoW also wanted. We will deploy FDEs to help with our models and to ensure their safety, we will deploy on cloud networks only.

We are asking the DoW to offer these same terms to all AI companies, which in our opinion we think everyone should be willing to accept. We have expressed our strong desire to see things de-escalate away from legal and governmental actions and towards reasonable agreements.

We remain committed to serve all of humanity as best we can. The world is a complicated, messy, and sometimes dangerous place.
items.author: @sama
items.summary: Building on yesterday's [Research](/?date=2026-02-28&category=research#item-275ec28ae0f5) coverage of Altman's internal memo, Sam Altman announces OpenAI reached agreement with the Department of War to deploy models on classified networks. States agreement includes prohibitions on domestic mass surveillance and requirements for human responsibility over use of force/autonomous weapons. Asks DoW to offer same terms to all AI companies.
items.reasoning: Breaking major news directly from OpenAI CEO with extraordinary engagement (31M+ views). This is a pivotal moment in AI-military integration policy and triggered massive public debate.

We are asking the DoW to offer these same terms to all AI companies, which in our opinion we think everyone should be willing to accept. We have expressed our strong desire to see things de-escalate away from legal and governmental actions and towards reasonable agreements.

Our agreement with the Department of War upholds our redlines:

- No use of OpenAI technology for ma...
items.content:
Our agreement with the Department of War upholds our redlines:

- No use of OpenAI technology for mass domestic surveillance.

- No use of OpenAI technology to direct autonomous weapons systems.

- No use of OpenAI technology for high-stakes automated decisions (e.g. systems such as “social credit”).

– Dario showed integrity and wanted deal X, and got screwed

items.content:
Let me get this straight. 

– Dario showed integrity and wanted deal X, and got screwed
- Sam lied and showed no integrity and got (pretty much) deal X
- The government said that deal X was appalling and severed ties with party A and then made almost the same deal with party B

This can’t be real, can it?
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus summarizes the situation: Dario showed integrity wanting deal X and got punished, Sam showed no integrity and got the same deal, and the government denounced terms from one party then accepted nearly identical terms from another.
items.reasoning: High engagement (67K views). Effectively frames the perceived hypocrisy of the entire situation.

items.content_html:
Let me get this straight.

– Dario showed integrity and wanted deal X, and got screwed

  • Sam lied and showed no integrity and got (pretty much) deal X
  • The government said that deal X was appalling and severed ties with party A and then made almost the same deal with party B

This can’t be real, can it?

items.title: Other AI labs have reduced or removed their safety guardrails and relied primarily on usage policies...
items.content:
Other AI labs have reduced or removed their safety guardrails and relied primarily on usage policies as their primary safeguards in national security deployments. We think our approach better protects against unacceptable use.

In our agreement, we protect our redlines through a more expansive, multi-layered approach. We retain full discretion over our safety stack, we deploy via cloud, cleared OpenAI personnel are in the loop, and we have strong contractual protections. This is all in addition to the strong existing protections in U.S. law.

items.title: @prajdabre @GhemawatSanjay Main lore around the origin of MapReduce was that we were rewriting our i...
items.content: @prajdabre @GhemawatSanjay Main lore around the origin of MapReduce was that we were rewriting our indexing pipeline for the search system, and we realized that lots of the different phases were conceptually simple but required large scale processing (extract link text from each page, identify language for each page, compute checksum of contents to identify duplicates, etc).  Each phase needed to be parallelized, made robust to machine failures, etc.  Squinting at each of the phases we came up with MapReduce as an abstraction where we could have an implementation that would do all the complex work under the abstraction boundary, and where the expression of the operations could be nice and simple.
items.author: @JeffDean
items.summary: Jeff Dean shares the origin story of MapReduce at Google — how they were rewriting the search indexing pipeline, noticed phases were conceptually simple but needed parallelization, and abstracted it into the MapReduce framework.
items.reasoning: Rare firsthand technical history from Jeff Dean (61.8K views, 1200 likes). Valuable primary source on one of the most influential distributed computing frameworks ever created.
items.summary_html: Jeff Dean shares the origin story of MapReduce at Google — how they were rewriting the search indexing pipeline, noticed phases were conceptually simple but needed parallelization, and abstracted it into the MapReduce framework.
items.content_html: @prajdabre @GhemawatSanjay Main lore around the origin of MapReduce was that we were rewriting our indexing pipeline for the search system, and we realized that lots of the different phases were conceptually simple but required large scale processing (extract link text from each page, identify language for each page, compute checksum of contents to identify duplicates, etc). Each phase needed to be parallelized, made robust to machine failures, etc. Squinting at each of the phases we came up with MapReduce as an abstraction where we could have an implementation that would do all the complex work under the abstraction boundary, and where the expression of the operations could be nice and simple.

items.title:
deep learning in 2013-~2020 was the OG vibe coding

1. read some arxiv papers, have no idea what wor...
items.content:
deep learning in 2013-~2020 was the OG vibe coding

1. read some arxiv papers, have no idea what works and what doesn't

2. so you just try out a bunch of random shit, with some inkling of what might work ("i'm just gonna sprinkle in some...batch norm, half the LR, skip connections 👩‍🍳") 

3. kick off like 20 runs across 500 gpus, wait a few hours/days 

4. one of these things magically works, you justify it as some huge breakthrough, dress it up with some numbers, publish it to cvpr/neurips
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) compares deep learning 2013-2020 to 'OG vibe coding': read papers without understanding what works, try random approaches with some intuition, run many experiments on GPUs, then dress up whatever works as a breakthrough.
items.reasoning: Highly engaging (42k views, 504 likes) and insightful cultural commentary from a credible AI industry figure. Draws amusing parallel between ML research and vibe coding, resonating widely.
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex CEO) compares deep learning 2013-2020 to 'OG vibe coding': read papers without understanding what works, try random approaches with some intuition, run many experiments on GPUs, then dress up whatever works as a breakthrough.

items.title:
@jerrod_lew @openclaw From my AI:

Here's the OpenClaw competitor landscape from across your 16 list...
items.content:
@jerrod_lew @openclaw From my AI:

Here's the OpenClaw competitor landscape from across your 16 lists:

Direct Clones / Forks:

MaxClaw (MiniMax) — "BREAKING: MiniMax launched MaxClaw, a new, always-on managed agent based on OpenClaw and powered by the MiniMax M2.5." The first major Chinese company to ship an OpenClaw-based managed agent. Widely RT'd across multiple lists.
om web app." Full ClawHub integration.

Kimi Claw (Moonshot/Kimi AI) — @WesRoth: "Kimi AI launches Kimi Claw 🦞, bringing the open-source OpenClaw agent framework directly into the📄 kimi.c

PravalClaw (@aiexplorations) — Fully Python-based, uses "Praval Agents" and "Reef communication" for the equivalent of OpenClaw lanes. Multiple connectors. Active development as of Feb 20.
Spiritual Successors / "OpenClaw but...":

Majordomo (@jasonthevictor) — "I got beat to market on the hosted openclaw thing... but today I'm shipping pre-built agents in Majordomo, and I think it's the coolest thing I've ever seen." Pre-built agent library approach.

Moonshift (@moonshiftio) — "OpenClaw showed what AI coding agents can do. We asked: what if the agent didn't stop at code? Moonshift takes it further." Positioned as post-coding execution layer.

opencode — @kaushikgopal: "@opencode is basically @openclaw for coding... an interesting focus area in the world of agent orchestration is 'access flow'." Coding-specific fork of the OpenClaw pattern.

Agent One (@PawelHuryn) — "RIP OpenClaw. Introducing Agent One: Autonomy with Security. Built in 3 days with Opus 4.6 and n8n." Security-first positioning.

YC-Backed / Funded:

emdash (ADE) — YC-backed, open-source Agentic Development Environment. "Run multiple coding agents in parallel." @ycombinator promoted. 115 likes. Founders: @rabanspiegel and @arnestrickmann.

Coding Agent Competitors Specifically:
@tylbar's unnamed agent — "Announcing a new coding agent that rivals Claude Code but with no compaction needed." Widely RT'd across AI Community #1, #2, #5. Positions directly against Claude Code (the coding layer of OpenClaw).

@dungle_scrubs open-source agent — "I built an open-source coding agent and stopped using Claude Code 4 days into it. Near full feature parity, multi-model." RT'd in AI Community #3.

Letta Code (Letta AI) — "Memory-first coding agent. Open source (Apache 2.0), model agnostic, portable agent learning." The memory angle is a specific differentiator.

The Pattern: Everyone is building either (a) a hosted managed version of OpenClaw, (b) a Chinese model-powered fork (MaxClaw, Kimi Claw), or (c) a coding-agent-specific variant. The OpenClaw framework itself is open source — what people are competing on is managed hosting, pre-built agents, and model integration. The "Agent Development Environment" (ADE) framing from emdash and others suggests that's the next evolution: OpenClaw as the IDE, not just the CLI.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer provides detailed landscape analysis of OpenClaw competitors including MaxClaw (MiniMax), Kimi Claw, PravalClaw, Majordomo, Moonshift, Agent One, emdash, and various coding agents. Notes the pattern of hosted versions, Chinese model forks, and coding-specific variants.
items.reasoning: Highly substantive and unique analysis of the AI agent framework ecosystem. Comprehensive competitive landscape covering OpenClaw forks, YC-backed alternatives, and Chinese model integrations. Maps the emerging 'Agent Development Environment' category. Generated by AI but curated/shared by a well-known tech commentator. This is the most information-dense post in the batch.

HUGE BREAKING NEWS: Why OpenAI and @sama could say yes where Anthropic couldn't.

Hint: it's architectural. OpenAI can say yes while Anthropic can't BECAUSE OF HOW THEY ARE BUILT.

HUGE analysis of all of X and everything is going on between OpenAI and Anthropic:

items.title:
OpenAI just raised $110B at a $730B valuation. SoftBank, NVIDIA, and Amazon each put in billions.

T...
items.content:
OpenAI just raised $110B at a $730B valuation. SoftBank, NVIDIA, and Amazon each put in billions.

The round includes $30B from SoftBank, $30B from NVIDIA, and $50B from Amazon.

OpenAI needs three things to scale: compute, distribution, and capital. This funding delivers all three.

The Amazon partnership brings:

> Global distribution through AWS infrastructure
> Enterprise reach across startups and governments
> Direct integration into Amazon's cloud services

The NVIDIA deal secures:

> 3 GW of dedicated inference capacity
> 2 GW of training on Vera Rubin systems
> Access to next-gen Hopper and Blackwell chips

OpenAI's products are already scaling fast:

1. Codex users tripled to 1.6M since January
2. ChatGPT hit 900M weekly active users
3. 50M consumer subscribers
4. 9M paying business users

The valuation pushes OpenAI Foundation's stake to $180B. That makes it one of the most well-funded nonprofits in history.

The bet: whoever scales infrastructure fastest wins the AI race. OpenAI just locked in the capital and compute to move first.

items.title:
Running dozens of fine-tuned MoE models on separate GPUs? Most of those GPUs are sitting idle.

Mult...
items.content:
Running dozens of fine-tuned MoE models on separate GPUs? Most of those GPUs are sitting idle.

Multi-LoRA serving for MoE models is now available in vLLM (since v0.15.0) — developed in collaboration with the @AmazonScience team. One base model, multiple LoRA adapters, one GPU.

📈 Results on GPT-OSS 20B:
- 454% higher output tokens/sec
- 87% lower time to first token
- Dense models benefit too: Qwen3 32B OTPS +99%

Under the hood: a new `fused_moe_lora` kernel that handles the compound sparsity of expert routing + adapter selection, plus Split-K for skinny matrix parallelism, CTA swizzling for L2 cache reuse, and fused LoRA weight addition.

Supports GPT-OSS, Qwen3-MoE, DeepSeek, and Llama MoE families. Verified on NVIDIA GPUs.

Blog: https://t.co/GdJJqOldUA

Thanks to the AWS contributors and the vLLM community members who made this happen 🙏
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM announces Multi-LoRA serving for MoE models (since v0.15.0), developed with Amazon. Achieves 454% higher output tokens/sec and 87% lower TTFT on GPT-OSS 20B through a fused_moe_lora kernel.
items.reasoning: Significant open-source infrastructure advancement. 11K views. Multi-LoRA MoE serving addresses real production needs, supports multiple model families.
items.summary_html: vLLM announces Multi-LoRA serving for MoE models (since v0.15.0), developed with Amazon. Achieves 454% higher output tokens/sec and 87% lower TTFT on GPT-OSS 20B through a fused_moe_lora kernel.
items.content_html:
Running dozens of fine-tuned MoE models on separate GPUs? Most of those GPUs are sitting idle.

Multi-LoRA serving for MoE models is now available in vLLM (since v0.15.0) — developed in collaboration with the @AmazonScience team. One base model, multiple LoRA adapters, one GPU.

📈 Results on GPT-OSS 20B:

  • 454% higher output tokens/sec
  • 87% lower time to first token
  • Dense models benefit too: Qwen3 32B OTPS +99%

Under the hood: a new `fused_moe_lora` kernel that handles the compound sparsity of expert routing + adapter selection, plus Split-K for skinny matrix parallelism, CTA swizzling for L2 cache reuse, and fused LoRA weight addition.

Supports GPT-OSS, Qwen3-MoE, DeepSeek, and Llama MoE families. Verified on NVIDIA GPUs.

Blog: https://t.co/GdJJqOldUA

Thanks to the AWS contributors and the vLLM community members who made this happen 🙏

items.title: @Scobleizer @sama what’s your source on these assertions?  I am not even sure it is coherent. and i ...
items.content:
@Scobleizer @sama what’s your source on these assertions?  I am not even sure it is coherent. and i would assume both companies use a wide variety of guardrails and alignment techniques. so i am not immediately finding this to be plausible.

“The deepest reason OpenAI could say yes while Anthropic couldn't is architectural.
Anthropicʼs Constitutional AI bakes refusals into Claude at training time. Claudeʼs resistance to autonomous weapons and mass surveillance isnʼt a rule applied on top — itʼs part of who Claude is. You canʼt system-prompt it away in a classified environment. The model structurally resists those requests regardless of context.
OpenAIʼs safety constraints live primarily in usage policies, system prompts, and RLHF tuning — layers that sit above the base model. In a classified deployment, those layers can be modified or removed by agreement. OpenAI can honestly say "our public models maintain safety commitments" while a classified variant operates without those specific constraints.
This means: Claude refuses these things because of who it is. GPT-4o refuses them because of rules it follows. In a classified environment with a contract, the rules can be renegotiated.”
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus questions Scobleizer's claim about architectural differences between OpenAI and Anthropic's safety approaches (Constitutional AI vs. RLHF layers), calling the distinction potentially incoherent.
items.reasoning: Substantively interesting technical discussion about whether safety in different AI systems is 'baked in' vs 'layered on,' even if Marcus is skeptical of the framing.
items.summary_html: Marcus questions Scobleizer's claim about architectural differences between OpenAI and Anthropic's safety approaches (Constitutional AI vs. RLHF layers), calling the distinction potentially incoherent.
items.content_html:
@Scobleizer @sama what’s your source on these assertions? I am not even sure it is coherent. and i would assume both companies use a wide variety of guardrails and alignment techniques. so i am not immediately finding this to be plausible.

“The deepest reason OpenAI could say yes while Anthropic couldn't is architectural.

Anthropicʼs Constitutional AI bakes refusals into Claude at training time. Claudeʼs resistance to autonomous weapons and mass surveillance isnʼt a rule applied on top — itʼs part of who Claude is. You canʼt system-prompt it away in a classified environment. The model structurally resists those requests regardless of context.

OpenAIʼs safety constraints live primarily in usage policies, system prompts, and RLHF tuning — layers that sit above the base model. In a classified deployment, those layers can be modified or removed by agreement. OpenAI can honestly say "our public models maintain safety commitments" while a classified variant operates without those specific constraints.

This means: Claude refuses these things because of who it is. GPT-4o refuses them because of rules it follows. In a classified environment with a contract, the rules can be renegotiated.”

items.title:
Imbue just open-sourced Evolver. A tool that uses LLMs to automatically optimize code and prompts.

...
items.content:
Imbue just open-sourced Evolver. A tool that uses LLMs to automatically optimize code and prompts.

They hit 95% on ARC-AGI-2 benchmarks. That's GPT-5.2-level performance from an open model.

Evolver works like natural selection for code.

You give it three things:

1. Starting code or prompt
2. A way to score results
3. An LLM that suggests improvements

Then it runs in a loop. It picks high-scoring solutions. Mutates them. Tests the mutations. Keeps what works.

The key difference from random mutation: LLMs propose targeted fixes.

When a solution fails on specific inputs, the LLM sees those failures. It suggests changes to fix them. Most suggestions don't help. But some do. Those survivors become parents for the next generation.

Evolver adds smart optimizations:

> Batch mutations: fix multiple failures at once
> Learning logs: share discoveries across branches
> Post-mutation filters: skip bad mutations before scoring

The verification step alone cuts costs 10x.

This works on any problem where LLMs can read the code and you can score the output.

You can now auto-optimize:
- Agentic workflows
- Prompt templates
- Code performance
- Reasoning chains

No gradient descent needed. No differentiable functions required.

items.title: @JohnProperBTC More importantly, industry tends to fund research that has identifiable applications ...

items.summary: Yann LeCun argues that fundamental long-term research (5-20 year horizon) only happens with government funding since industry focuses on short/medium-term applications.

items.summary_html: Yann LeCun argues that fundamental long-term research (5-20 year horizon) only happens with government funding since industry focuses on short/medium-term applications.
items.content_html:
@JohnProperBTC More importantly, industry tends to fund research that has identifiable applications in the short to medium term.

Long-term research in industry is quite rare.

So, fundamental research, which often takes 5, 10 or 20 years to have product impact, only happens if governments fund it.

items.title: @UnderSecretaryF @AnthropicAI So will Anthropic be (now) offered the same terms that OpenAI just acc...
items.content:
@UnderSecretaryF @AnthropicAI So will Anthropic be (now) offered the same terms that OpenAI just accepted?

And will those terms be made public?

Unless the answers to both questions are yes, I maintain that this looks really really bad.

It’s fine that the USG didn’t accept Anthropic’s terms, but a presidential smackdown preventing any future business from them simply because the parties couldn’t come to terms is AFAIK unprecedented.

The right thing to do is to come back to Anthropic with the terms that OpenAI just struck.

items.title:
Key design choices:

KV cache layout redesigned around AMD's CDNA architecture.
Decode ops skip layo...
items.content:
Key design choices:

KV cache layout redesigned around AMD's CDNA architecture.
Decode ops skip layout conversion entirely → 15-20% gain.

For DeepSeek MLA: ROCM_AITER_MLA compresses KV cache from ~8K → 576 dims (14x reduction) with hand-tuned assembly decode kernels.

Benchmarks across MI300X / MI325X / MI355X (64 concurrent requests):

MHA (Qwen3-235B): 2.7-4.4x TPS over legacy ROCM_ATTN
MLA (DeepSeek-R1): 1.2-1.5x TPS over Triton baseline

Consistent across 3 GPU generations.
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM details AMD ROCM optimizations: redesigned KV cache layout for CDNA architecture, DeepSeek MLA 14x KV cache compression, with 2.7-4.4x TPS gains across MI300X/MI325X/MI355X.
items.reasoning: Deep technical content about AMD GPU optimization for LLM serving. Important for the AMD AI ecosystem. 1.4K views.
items.summary_html: vLLM details AMD ROCM optimizations: redesigned KV cache layout for CDNA architecture, DeepSeek MLA 14x KV cache compression, with 2.7-4.4x TPS gains across MI300X/MI325X/MI355X.
items.content_html:

For DeepSeek MLA: ROCM_AITER_MLA compresses KV cache from ~8K → 576 dims (14x reduction) with hand-tuned assembly decode kernels.

Benchmarks across MI300X / MI325X / MI355X (64 concurrent requests):

MHA (Qwen3-235B): 2.7-4.4x TPS over legacy ROCM_ATTN

MLA (DeepSeek-R1): 1.2-1.5x TPS over Triton baseline

Consistent across 3 GPU generations.

items.title:
LLM serving mixes 3 fundamentally different workloads per batch:
- Prefill — thousands of tokens, co...
items.content:
LLM serving mixes 3 fundamentally different workloads per batch:
- Prefill — thousands of tokens, compute-bound
- Decode — single token, memory-bandwidth-bound
- Extend — cached + fresh context, both

ROCM_AITER_FA uses explicit 3-path routing — each phase gets its own specialized kernel.
items.author: @vllm_project
items.summary: vLLM explains 3-path kernel routing for LLM serving (prefill/decode/extend), with ROCM_AITER_FA providing specialized kernels for each phase.
items.reasoning: Technical deep-dive into LLM serving architecture optimization. Complements the broader vLLM AMD announcement.
items.summary_html: vLLM explains 3-path kernel routing for LLM serving (prefill/decode/extend), with ROCM_AITER_FA providing specialized kernels for each phase.
items.content_html:
LLM serving mixes 3 fundamentally different workloads per batch:

  • Prefill — thousands of tokens, compute-bound
  • Decode — single token, memory-bandwidth-bound
  • Extend — cached + fresh context, both

ROCM_AITER_FA uses explicit 3-path routing — each phase gets its own specialized kernel.

items.title: Tonight, we reached an agreement with the Department of War to deploy our models in their classified...
items.content:
Tonight, we reached an agreement with the Department of War to deploy our models in their classified network.

In all of our interactions, the DoW displayed a deep respect for safety and a desire to partner to achieve the best possible outcome.

AI safety and wide distribution of benefits are the core of our mission. Two of our most important safety principles are prohibitions on domestic mass surveillance and human responsibility for the use of force, including for autonomous weapon systems.  The DoW agrees with these principles, reflects them in law and policy, and we put them into our agreement.

We also will build technical safeguards to ensure our models behave as they should, which the DoW also wanted. We will deploy FDEs to help with our models and to ensure their safety, we will deploy on cloud networks only.

We are asking the DoW to offer these same terms to all AI companies, which in our opinion we think everyone should be willing to accept. We have expressed our strong desire to see things de-escalate away from legal and governmental actions and towards reasonable agreements.

We remain committed to serve all of humanity as best we can. The world is a complicated, messy, and sometimes dangerous place.

The DoW agrees with these principles, reflects them in law and policy, and we put them into our agreement.

items.title: Tonight, we reached an agreement with the Department of War to deploy our models in their classified...
items.content:
Tonight, we reached an agreement with the Department of War to deploy our models in their classified network.

In all of our interactions, the DoW displayed a deep respect for safety and a desire to partner to achieve the best possible outcome.

AI safety and wide distribution of benefits are the core of our mission. Two of our most important safety principles are prohibitions on domestic mass surveillance and human responsibility for the use of force, including for autonomous weapon systems.  The DoW agrees with these principles, reflects them in law and policy, and we put them into our agreement.

We also will build technical safeguards to ensure our models behave as they should, which the DoW also wanted. We will deploy FDEs to help with our models and to ensure their safety, we will deploy on cloud networks only.

We are asking the DoW to offer these same terms to all AI companies, which in our opinion we think everyone should be willing to accept. We have expressed our strong desire to see things de-escalate away from legal and governmental actions and towards reasonable agreements.

We remain committed to serve all of humanity as best we can. The world is a complicated, messy, and sometimes dangerous place.

Every time your agent makes a mistake, take a second to build a solution so it never happens again.

It takes a second to ask your agent to analyze what happened and propose a solution.

This investment today will compound over time. Every day, your agents will become better.

items.title: 🌶️ I don’t think people should hire others to do AI work unless they are using AI heavily in their o...
items.content:
🌶️ I don’t think people should hire others to do AI work unless they are using AI heavily in their own daily workflows

“I use ChatGPT once in a while” doesn’t count 

Without this:
- You’ll make bad decisions and have too many blind spots 
- It’s a sign you aren’t really invested in AI and are trying to check a box 

I think it’s totally fine to hire experts if you are in this position if you also upskill at the same time, but delegating AI work and continuing to stay at arms length from AI you are ngmi
items.author: @HamelHusain
items.summary: Hamel Husain argues people shouldn't hire others for AI work unless they themselves use AI heavily in daily workflows. Simply 'using ChatGPT once in a while' doesn't count.
items.reasoning: Practical advice from an experienced ML practitioner about organizational AI adoption. Moderate engagement. Actionable insight for leaders.

`vllm serve <model>`

items.content:
Getting started:

`export VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER=1`
`vllm serve <model>`

Auto-selects the right backend for your model. One env var, no code changes.

Full technical breakdown 🔗 https://t.co/DuZYFptgzn

Thanks to @AIatAMD and @EmbeddedLLM!

items.title: The problem with "all lawful use cases" is that most of those laws were written way before we had an...
items.content: The problem with "all lawful use cases" is that most of those laws were written way before we had any idea what these AI tools were capable of. Things that no one could conceive of are now mundane reality. Laws have always been way behind the technology, and at this point they will never catch up with it.

items.title: Get "Mathematics of Machine Learning" here: https://t.co/07exFk5LqL by @TivadarDanka v/ @PacktDataML...
items.content:
Get "Mathematics of Machine Learning" here: https://t.co/07exFk5LqL by @TivadarDanka v/ @PacktDataML 
—
GitHub: https://t.co/2ENjzhr35C
—
Here is my review:

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗲𝘁 𝗢𝗳 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗹𝗴𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗺𝘀 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗻 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲

This massive book is incredible, with its comprehensive coverage of numerous fields of mathematics and their intersection with the world of AI, data science, and machine learning (AI+DSML). I remember the very first time that I encountered machine learning. This was 20+ years ago, and that was already after 20+ years of being drenched in advanced mathematics as an astrophysicist. 

That first encounter of mine with ML was this definition: "Machine learning is the set of mathematical algorithms that learn from experience" (slightly paraphrased from the original quote by Tom Mitchell, CMU). That definition surprised me, confused me, motivated me, and changed the course of my career from astrophysics into AI+DSML. 

This book by Tivadar Danka captures the full meaning of that definition. The book covers thoroughly the many areas and domains of mathematics through which patterns in data are detected, described, learned, and recognized - all for the benefit of powering ML and AI algorithms, applications, and aspirations. 

This book will motivate you, surprise you, and inspire you in many ways, no matter what level of mathematics has (or has not) already propelled your career journey. There is room for all of us to grow. 

This is a great book, worthy to sit on everyone's desktop, ready to help you explore and exploit the full set of mathematical algorithms that learn from experience. 

The book is accompanied by a rich GitHub code repository of Jupyter notebooks. Learn by doing! Do by learning!

Disclosure: the publisher provided me with a free review copy of the book.

A new mini-app on https://t.co/Qj9rYqJNuu: Cover letter generator

The user uploads their resume, pa...
items.content:
A new mini-app on https://t.co/Qj9rYqJNuu: Cover letter generator

The user uploads their resume, pastes a link to the job description, choses the length and the style of the letter, and gets the letter generated.

The user's resume and the extracted parameters can be saved for future use.

The link to the cover letter generator: https://t.co/LRC1UkcWrP

Also, don't forget about the app that tailors your resume to a job description: https://t.co/AUaysofZTg

@LaLiga @Globant @SportIan @StatsPerform @monks @F1 @Lenovo 5️⃣ How NASCAR Uses GPU-Accelerated Simulation for Safer, More Exciting Racing 
📅 Wed Mar 18 | 3:00 PM PDT

CFD now accounts for 80% of all aero development at @NASCAR.

By moving from CPU-based CFD to CUDA-X accelerated workflows on NVIDIA GB200 GPUs, they're seeing 10x faster turnaround — enabling bigger, higher-fidelity multi-physics models for safety and performance.

Available at https://t.co/uwuobKTPm3

This LLM book is for database developers and data scientists w...
items.content:
Available at https://t.co/uwuobKTPm3

This LLM book is for database developers and data scientists who want to leverage knowledge graphs with Neo4j and its vector search capabilities to build intelligent search and recommendation systems. Working knowledge of Python and Java is essential to follow along. Familiarity with Neo4j, the Cypher query language, and fundamental concepts of databases will come in handy.

𝓦𝓱𝓪𝓽 𝓨𝓸𝓾 𝓦𝓲𝓵𝓵 𝓛𝓮𝓪𝓻𝓷:
🟠Design, populate, and integrate a Neo4j knowledge graph with RAG
🔵Model data for knowledge graphs
🟢Integrate AI-powered search to enhance knowledge exploration
🟠Maintain and monitor your AI search application with Haystack
🔵Use LangChain4j and Spring AI for recommendations and personalization
🟢Seamlessly deploy your applications to Google Cloud Platform

The real shift is not automation.
It’s agentic demos.

→ An AI agent sits behind your site, app, or outbound link

→ It walks the product, shares screens, answers questions in real time

No waiting.
No scheduling.
No SDR bottleneck.

items.title:
my energy in the past month: slow, fluctuating progress!

observations:
- past week I had 2-3 consec...
items.content:
my energy in the past month: slow, fluctuating progress!

observations:
- past week I had 2-3 consecutive days of good energy! then crashed a bit :(
- I'm "crashing" like every 3-4 days on average, but that's lengthening
- the stack of butyrate + omega-3 plasmalogen + phosphatidylcholine can reliably pull me out of a 5 into 5.5-6 range

what the numbers mean:
- 5 is like tired and I'd rather stay in bed but can force myself to do things if I have to
- 6 is like I can do things I need to but my brain is kinda empty and slow
- 7 is like I don't feel any resistance to do the things I need to do
- 8 is like that but my brain is running noticeably faster than 7
items.author: @TheGregYang
items.summary: Greg Yang shares personal health/energy tracking data and supplement stack (butyrate, omega-3 plasmalogen, phosphatidylcholine).
items.reasoning: Off-topic personal health post from an ML researcher. Not AI/ML related.
items.summary_html: Greg Yang shares personal health/energy tracking data and supplement stack (butyrate, omega-3 plasmalogen, phosphatidylcholine).
items.content_html:
my energy in the past month: slow, fluctuating progress!

observations:

  • past week I had 2-3 consecutive days of good energy! then crashed a bit :(
  • I'm "crashing" like every 3-4 days on average, but that's lengthening
  • the stack of butyrate + omega-3 plasmalogen + phosphatidylcholine can reliably pull me out of a 5 into 5.5-6 range

what the numbers mean:

  • 5 is like tired and I'd rather stay in bed but can force myself to do things if I have to
  • 6 is like I can do things I need to but my brain is kinda empty and slow
  • 7 is like I don't feel any resistance to do the things I need to do
  • 8 is like that but my brain is running noticeably faster than 7

items.title:
be careful about blueberry skin

I've long felt blueberries's anti inflammatory effect but also an i...
items.content:
be careful about blueberry skin

I've long felt blueberries's anti inflammatory effect but also an inflammatory effect at the same time (they don't cancel out)

today finally I cut some open and ate only the flesh --- voila purely anti inflammatory

when i ate the skin itself it's purely inflammatory

not sure why but potentially pesticides
items.author: @TheGregYang
items.summary: Greg Yang warns about blueberry skins being inflammatory while blueberry flesh is anti-inflammatory, speculates about pesticides.
items.reasoning: Completely off-topic from AI/ML. Personal health observation.
items.summary_html: Greg Yang warns about blueberry skins being inflammatory while blueberry flesh is anti-inflammatory, speculates about pesticides.

be careful about blueberry skin

today finally I cut some open and ate only the flesh --- voila purely anti inflammatory

when i ate the skin itself it's purely inflammatory

not sure why but potentially pesticides

items.content: @SarthakPar16 Of course it's a spectrum, but I'm starting to think that this is true for most people. If the range of appreciation is from 1-10, I would guess a majority are <=3 (no to minimal appreciation, at the level of light entertainment and distraction, certainly not Bach fugues, etc.)

items.title: Sometimes people proudly announce to me that they "don't get music", that it "does nothing for them"...
items.content: Sometimes people proudly announce to me that they "don't get music", that it "does nothing for them". I feel truly bad for that. It's another sense, another dimension. For me, nothing else is as moving or thought provoking. The most beautiful writing is as close as it gets.
